site.name: The Late Screening
site.description: A compact static archive of contemporary film reviews written in a critic's voice.

about.name: Mara Voss
about.bio: Mara Voss is the fictional critic behind The Late Screening, a one-person review site built around the idea that a movie's meaning is inseparable from its form. She writes from Rotterdam, keeps late cinema hours, and tends to care as much about rhythm, framing, and music cues as she does about plot. The archive began as a private notebook after the long stop-start years of moviegoing and gradually became a public habit: one review at a time, filed after festival screenings, commercial openings, and weekend catch-up viewings. Mara's taste is broad but not indiscriminate. She has a weakness for ambitious genre filmmaking, patient dramas, and movies that trust images more than exposition. Just as often, she writes about disappointments, especially when a film has craft to spare but no clear dramatic center. What ties the site together is not a single ideology so much as a method: close attention to acting, direction, screenplay, pacing, cinematography, and music, plus a refusal to flatten everything into thumbs-up consensus. The Late Screening is meant to feel like one critic's running conversation with the last few years of cinema—enthusiastic, skeptical, and consistent enough that agreement is less important than clarity.

reviews.title: The Teachers' Lounge
reviews.review:
The Teachers' Lounge is easy to admire in pieces even when the whole never quite settles into a persuasive rhythm. There are strong performances here, especially when the film allows silence to do the work, yet several scenes feel performed toward the camera rather than toward one another. İlker Çatak has an eye for moments but not always for proportion. The direction is alert scene to scene, then suddenly indulgent when the film most needs compression. The screenplay is at its best in the smaller interactions; whenever it strains toward thesis, the dialogue grows conspicuously literary.

Pacing is where admiration and frustration most directly meet. The film has stretches of real momentum, then wanders into repetitions that sap force from what came before. There are memorable images throughout, though the look occasionally feels imported from a stronger movie than the screenplay provides. Musically the film is polished, but the score occasionally insists on importance where a cooler touch would have served better. In criticism, that kind of partial success can be more revealing than simple failure: it shows exactly where craft begins and judgment falters.

The result is an engagingly imperfect film, one whose flaws are visible but whose ambitions are never trivial. It may not fully cohere, yet it gives a critic plenty to think about on the walk home.

reviews.title: Past Lives
reviews.review:
Few recent releases feel as fully considered as Past Lives, a work that wears its ideas lightly but leaves a deep impression. Acting is one of the film's quiet triumphs, with the ensemble finding texture instead of merely underlining meaning; characters reveal themselves by degrees, and that restraint pays dividends. Celine Song directs with a calm, almost architectural intelligence, shaping tone through rhythm and framing rather than through blunt declarations. The screenplay is sharper than it first appears, circling its ideas through recurring images and offhand exchanges that gather meaning over time.

Its pacing is measured but never inert. The film knows when to linger for atmosphere and when to cut away before a point begins to harden into explanation. Visually, the film is a pleasure. The cinematography finds expressive uses for scale, negative space, and texture, turning mood into something tactile. Musically, the film is alert to cadence. The score supports tension and release without flattening either into obvious cues. A better sense of selection—what to withhold, what to stress, what to leave for the image to carry—might have transformed the same material.

In the end, Past Lives earns its praise by remaining curious about its own people and formal enough to give that curiosity shape. It is rare to see a film this controlled still feel this alive.

reviews.title: The Taste of Things
reviews.review:
Few recent releases feel as fully considered as The Taste of Things, a work that wears its ideas lightly but leaves a deep impression. The cast works at a register just beneath obvious emphasis, which lets the drama breathe. Reactions matter as much as speeches, and the movie is better for it. As a director, Trần Anh Hùng understands how to organize space, emotion, and information so that the film keeps unfolding instead of merely advancing. On the page, the film is unusually attentive to contradiction: characters can be funny and guarded, vain and wounded, lucid and self-deceiving, often in the same scene.

The rhythm is one of the film's major strengths, moving with assurance through tonal shifts that might have defeated a less attentive edit. The cinematography does more than supply beauty; it clarifies feeling. Light, distance, and camera placement become part of the storytelling rather than decoration around it. The music is used with admirable restraint, entering to color a scene rather than bully it, and the sound design is equally attentive to silence.

What finally lingers is not just craft but confidence: The Taste of Things knows what it wants to say and has found a cinematic language sturdy enough to say it. It is a fully persuasive piece of criticism-through-storytelling, and one of the more memorable films in this archive.

reviews.title: May December
reviews.review:
May December has the unmistakable shape of a film made with conviction, even if that conviction sometimes hardens into self-importance. The acting does much of the heavy lifting, though not every performance belongs to the same movie. Some scenes crackle with feeling while others settle for demonstration rather than revelation. Todd Haynes keeps the film watchable through sheer command of texture, though the staging cannot always disguise structural hesitation. There are strong ideas in the writing, though the script tends to announce them before it has fully dramatized them.

The edit finds a workable flow, though the middle act noticeably loosens its grip and asks patience it has not entirely earned. There are memorable images throughout, though the look occasionally feels imported from a stronger movie than the screenplay provides. The score is effective in isolation, less so when it begins to compensate for narrative gaps that direction and writing leave exposed. That tension between ambition and execution becomes the defining quality of the experience, and it is why the film stays discussable long after its weaker choices have cooled.

The result is an engagingly imperfect film, one whose flaws are visible but whose ambitions are never trivial. It may not fully cohere, yet it gives a critic plenty to think about on the walk home.

reviews.review:

reviews.title: Janet Planet
reviews.review:
There is enough intelligence in Janet Planet to hold attention, though not always enough discipline to justify every detour. Annie Baker keeps the film watchable through sheer command of texture, though the staging cannot always disguise structural hesitation. reviews.title: Gladiator II
reviews.review:
Gladiator II mistakes heaviness for depth, and the confusion weighs down nearly every scene. Even capable actors cannot quite rescue the thin characterization, and the emotional beats arrive as instructions rather than discoveries. Ridley Scott reaches for grandeur but too often settles for emphasis, substituting volume and visual clutter for genuine accumulation. Writing is the movie's central weakness: scenes repeat the same information, motivations remain schematic, and conflict arrives prepackaged.

Its rhythm is fatally misjudged, with long passages of setup followed by abrupt emotional shortcuts. The images aim for significance but too often resemble advertising for emotions the film has not earned. Music is used as a substitute for dramatic tension, a recurring sign that the film does not trust its own images. That tension between ambition and execution becomes the defining quality of the experience, and it is why the film stays discussable long after its weaker choices have cooled. That is often the difference between an arresting misfire and a merely dutiful one, and this film sits uncomfortably between the two.

For all its effort, Gladiator II never becomes more than intermittently interesting. A few isolated choices work, but the overall design is too strained and too thin to recommend strongly.

reviews.title: Rustin
reviews.review:
George C. Wolfe reaches for something substantial in Rustin, and the result is as intriguing as it is uneven. The acting does much of the heavy lifting, though not every performance belongs to the same movie. Some scenes crackle with feeling while others settle for demonstration rather than revelation. George C. Wolfe has an eye for moments but not always for proportion. The direction is alert scene to scene, then suddenly indulgent when the film most needs compression. The screenplay is at its best in the smaller interactions; whenever it strains toward thesis, the dialogue grows conspicuously literary.

The edit finds a workable flow, though the middle act noticeably loosens its grip and asks patience it has not entirely earned. There are memorable images throughout, though the look occasionally feels imported from a stronger movie than the screenplay provides. The score is effective in isolation, less so when it begins to compensate for narrative gaps that direction and writing leave exposed. A better sense of selection—what to withhold, what to stress, what to leave for the image to carry—might have transformed the same material.

I admired Rustin more than I loved it, which is not a small distinction but not a dismissal either. The film remains compelling precisely because its strengths and weaknesses are so closely entangled.

reviews.title: Babygirl
reviews.review:
Babygirl mistakes heaviness for depth, and the confusion weighs down nearly every scene. Even capable actors cannot quite rescue the thin characterization, and the emotional beats arrive as instructions rather than discoveries. As direction, the film feels overmanaged on the surface and underimagined underneath, a poor combination for drama of any scale. The screenplay reduces complexity to a series of neat signals, so the film keeps telling us what matters instead of creating conditions for us to feel it.

Pacing is a recurring problem. Scenes overstay, transitions blunt momentum, and the cumulative effect is less hypnotic than simply draggy. For all the polish, the cinematography feels curiously anonymous, giving the film a finish without much point of view. Music is used as a substitute for dramatic tension, a recurring sign that the film does not trust its own images. Even when I resisted the film, I could still see the outline of the better picture inside it, especially in the smaller scenes where texture briefly replaces thesis. What makes the disappointment sharper is that the film occasionally hints at a tougher, more observant version of itself, one willing to trust the audience rather than keep signaling every intention.

What remains after the credits is mostly the sense of squandered possibility. Babygirl has resources, ideas, and occasional style, but too little dramatic judgment to turn them into a satisfying whole.

reviews.title: Better Man
reviews.review:
There is enough intelligence in Better Man to hold attention, though not always enough discipline to justify every detour. There are strong performances here, especially when the film allows silence to do the work, yet several scenes feel performed toward the camera rather than toward one another. Michael Gracey has an eye for moments but not always for proportion. The direction is alert scene to scene, then suddenly indulgent when the film most needs compression. There are strong ideas in the writing, though the script tends to announce them before it has fully dramatized them.

The film never fully collapses, but its rhythm is inconsistent: brisk in setup, slack in development, and hurried when consequences finally arrive. The camera often knows where the film's emotional center should be, but the surrounding visual design is not always equally purposeful. Musically the film is polished, but the score occasionally insists on importance where a cooler touch would have served better. The movie is not devoid of craft, but craft without proportion can only take a picture so far.

I admired Better Man more than I loved it, which is not a small distinction but not a dismissal either. The film remains compelling precisely because its strengths and weaknesses are so closely entangled.

reviews.title: Nickel Boys
reviews.review:
Nickel Boys is the kind of film that trusts form as much as plot, and that faith pays off more often than not. Acting is one of the film's quiet triumphs, with the ensemble finding texture instead of merely underlining meaning; characters reveal themselves by degrees, and that restraint pays dividends. RaMell Ross directs with a calm, almost architectural intelligence, shaping tone through rhythm and framing rather than through blunt declarations. The screenplay is sharper than it first appears, circling its ideas through recurring images and offhand exchanges that gather meaning over time.

Its pacing is measured but never inert. The film knows when to linger for atmosphere and when to cut away before a point begins to harden into explanation. Image-making matters here. The framing is exact, the color design coherent, and the visual motifs strong enough to carry thought between scenes. The score understands the value of pressure applied lightly, shaping mood while leaving room for the actors and the frame to keep speaking.

What finally lingers is not just craft but confidence: Nickel Boys knows what it wants to say and has found a cinematic language sturdy enough to say it. It is a fully persuasive piece of criticism-through-storytelling, and one of the more memorable films in this archive.

Oppenheimer arrives with the confidence of a filmmaker who knows exactly where every image should land. The cast works at a register just beneath obvious emphasis, which lets the drama breathe. Reactions matter as much as speeches, and the movie is better for it. Christopher Nolan's direction is precise without becoming fussy. Scenes enter late, exit cleanly, and accumulate into something larger than their individual mechanics. On the page, the film is unusually attentive to contradiction: characters can be funny and guarded, vain and wounded, lucid and self-deceiving, often in the same scene.

The rhythm is one of the film's major strengths, moving with assurance through tonal shifts that might have defeated a less attentive edit. The cinematography does more than supply beauty; it clarifies feeling. Light, distance, and camera placement become part of the storytelling rather than decoration around it. Musically, the film is alert to cadence. The score supports tension and release without flattening either into obvious cues. In criticism, that kind of partial success can be more revealing than simple failure: it shows exactly where craft begins and judgment falters.

In the end, Oppenheimer earns its praise by remaining curious about its own people and formal enough to give that curiosity shape. It is rare to see a film this controlled still feel this alive.

reviews.title: Nightbitch
reviews.review:
For all its noise, Nightbitch feels oddly hollow, as though its surface were finished long before its inner life was written. Even capable actors cannot quite rescue the thin characterization, and the emotional beats arrive as instructions rather than discoveries. As direction, the film feels overmanaged on the surface and underimagined underneath, a poor combination for drama of any scale. The screenplay reduces complexity to a series of neat signals, so the film keeps telling us what matters instead of creating conditions for us to feel it.

The movie lumbers from beat to beat, mistaking delay for tension and length for seriousness. The images aim for significance but too often resemble advertising for emotions the film has not earned. The soundtrack keeps announcing significance, which only calls attention to how little weight many scenes actually carry. In criticism, that kind of partial success can be more revealing than simple failure: it shows exactly where craft begins and judgment falters. A better sense of selection—what to withhold, what to stress, what to leave for the image to carry—might have transformed the same material.

What remains after the credits is mostly the sense of squandered possibility. Nightbitch has resources, ideas, and occasional style, but too little dramatic judgment to turn them into a satisfying whole.

reviews.title: Beau Is Afraid
reviews.review:

Ari Aster directs with a calm, almost architectural intelligence, shaping tone through rhythm and framing rather than through blunt declarations. The screenplay is sharper than it first appears, circling its ideas through recurring images and offhand exchanges that gather meaning over time.

The framing is exact, the color design coherent, and the visual motifs strong enough to carry thought between scenes. reviews.title: Inside Out 2
reviews.review:

The cast remains committed even when the script corners them into explanation. Individual moments land, but the collective rhythm is less assured. Kelsey Mann keeps the film watchable through sheer command of texture, though the staging cannot always disguise structural hesitation. The screenplay has genuine insight but also a weakness for overstatement. It can sketch a vivid situation, then stop to explain what the audience has already understood.

Pacing is where admiration and frustration most directly meet. The film has stretches of real momentum, then wanders into repetitions that sap force from what came before. There are memorable images throughout, though the look occasionally feels imported from a stronger movie than the screenplay provides. Musically the film is polished, but the score occasionally insists on importance where a cooler touch would have served better. What makes the disappointment sharper is that the film occasionally hints at a tougher, more observant version of itself, one willing to trust the audience rather than keep signaling every intention.

The result is an engagingly imperfect film, one whose flaws are visible but whose ambitions are never trivial. It may not fully cohere, yet it gives a critic plenty to think about on the walk home.

reviews.title: Blitz
reviews.review:
Blitz is never short on intention, but intention alone cannot disguise how thinly the film is finally imagined. The cast appears stranded between sincerity and camp, with performances that register as effortful when they should feel lived in. Steve McQueen reaches for grandeur but too often settles for emphasis, substituting volume and visual clutter for genuine accumulation. The screenplay reduces complexity to a series of neat signals, so the film keeps telling us what matters instead of creating conditions for us to feel it.

Pacing is a recurring problem. Scenes overstay, transitions blunt momentum, and the cumulative effect is less hypnotic than simply draggy. For all the polish, the cinematography feels curiously anonymous, giving the film a finish without much point of view. The score works overtime, pushing scenes toward feeling they have not built for themselves and leaving little room for ambiguity. That is often the difference between an arresting misfire and a merely dutiful one, and this film sits uncomfortably between the two. That tension between ambition and execution becomes the defining quality of the experience, and it is why the film stays discussable long after its weaker choices have cooled.

By the final stretch, Blitz feels less like a finished argument than a collection of emphatic gestures. There are flashes of ability, but they are swallowed by a film that mistakes burden for depth.

For all the polish, the cinematography feels curiously anonymous, giving the film a finish without much point of view. The score works overtime, pushing scenes toward feeling they have not built for themselves and leaving little room for ambiguity. Babylon mistakes heaviness for depth, and the confusion weighs down nearly every scene. The acting rarely finds a persuasive center. Performers push hard for urgency, but the screenplay gives them mostly attitudes and exposition to play. The screenplay reduces complexity to a series of neat signals, so the film keeps telling us what matters instead of creating conditions for us to feel it.

Pacing is a recurring problem. Scenes overstay, transitions blunt momentum, and the cumulative effect is less hypnotic than simply draggy. For all the polish, the cinematography feels curiously anonymous, giving the film a finish without much point of view. The score works overtime, pushing scenes toward feeling they have not built for themselves and leaving little room for ambiguity. That is often the difference between an arresting misfire and a merely dutiful one, and this film sits uncomfortably between the two. That tension between ambition and execution becomes the defining quality of the experience, and it is why the film stays discussable long after its weaker choices have cooled.

What remains after the credits is mostly the sense of squandered possibility. Babylon has resources, ideas, and occasional style, but too little dramatic judgment to turn them into a satisfying whole.

reviews.title: Eternals
reviews.review:
For all its noise, Eternals feels oddly hollow, as though its surface were finished long before its inner life was written. The acting rarely finds a persuasive center. Performers push hard for urgency, but the screenplay gives them mostly attitudes and exposition to play. As direction, the film feels overmanaged on the surface and underimagined underneath, a poor combination for drama of any scale. Writing is the movie's central weakness: scenes repeat the same information, motivations remain schematic, and conflict arrives prepackaged.

Pacing is a recurring problem. Scenes overstay, transitions blunt momentum, and the cumulative effect is less hypnotic than simply draggy. Visually, the film alternates between generic competence and self-conscious flourish, neither of which deepens the drama. The score works overtime, pushing scenes toward feeling they have not built for themselves and leaving little room for ambiguity. A better sense of selection—what to withhold, what to stress, what to leave for the image to carry—might have transformed the same material. The movie is not devoid of craft, but craft without proportion can only take a picture so far.

By the final stretch, Eternals feels less like a finished argument than a collection of emphatic gestures. There are flashes of ability, but they are swallowed by a film that mistakes burden for depth.

The screenplay is at its best in the smaller interactions; whenever it strains toward thesis, the dialogue grows conspicuously literary.

reviews.title: The Souvenir Part II
reviews.review:
Joanna Hogg mounts The Souvenir Part II with evident seriousness, yet the movie struggles to turn design into drama. Even capable actors cannot quite rescue the thin characterization, and the emotional beats arrive as instructions rather than discoveries. Joanna Hogg reaches for grandeur but too often settles for emphasis, substituting volume and visual clutter for genuine accumulation. The screenplay reaches for gravity with a great many speeches and not nearly enough observation.

Pacing is a recurring problem. Scenes overstay, transitions blunt momentum, and the cumulative effect is less hypnotic than simply draggy. Visually, the film alternates between generic competence and self-conscious flourish, neither of which deepens the drama. What makes the disappointment sharper is that the film occasionally hints at a tougher, more observant version of itself, one willing to trust the audience rather than keep signaling every intention. In criticism, that kind of partial success can be more revealing than simple failure: it shows exactly where craft begins and judgment falters.

What remains after the credits is mostly the sense of squandered possibility. The Souvenir Part II has resources, ideas, and occasional style, but too little dramatic judgment to turn them into a satisfying whole.

reviews.title: King Richard
reviews.review:
King Richard is the kind of film that trusts form as much as plot, and that faith pays off more often than not. Acting is one of the film's quiet triumphs, with the ensemble finding texture instead of merely underlining meaning; characters reveal themselves by degrees, and that restraint pays dividends. Reinaldo Marcus Green directs with a calm, almost architectural intelligence, shaping tone through rhythm and framing rather than through blunt declarations. The screenplay is sharper than it first appears, circling its ideas through recurring images and offhand exchanges that gather meaning over time.

The rhythm is one of the film's major strengths, moving with assurance through tonal shifts that might have defeated a less attentive edit. The cinematography does more than supply beauty; it clarifies feeling. Light, distance, and camera placement become part of the storytelling rather than decoration around it. Musically, the film is alert to cadence. The score supports tension and release without flattening either into obvious cues. That is often the difference between an arresting misfire and a merely dutiful one, and this film sits uncomfortably between the two.

In the end, King Richard earns its praise by remaining curious about its own people and formal enough to give that curiosity shape. It is rare to see a film this controlled still feel this alive.
